name: Participant Recruitment Strategy
consequences: Recruiting participants through existing medical networks (University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals) may yield a higher success rate due to established trust and referral pathways. However, relying on these channels might limit the participant pool's diversity.
options: Targeted social media campaigns with patient advocacy groups
Collaborate with local community centers for outreach and education
Partner with sleep disorder support groups for referrals
review: This lever manages the tension between leveraging established medical networks and exploring alternative recruitment channels, but the options overlook potential participant fatigue from repeated solicitations.

name: Sensor Data Management
consequences: Implementing a tiered acquisition model with nightly low-burden monitoring may reduce participant burden, but it might compromise data quality due to reduced sampling rates.
options: Integrate machine learning algorithms for automated event detection and prioritization
Use edge computing for real-time processing and filtering of sensor data
Develop a hybrid approach combining tiered acquisition with full PSG on select nights
review: This lever governs the trade-off between participant comfort and data quality, but the options overlook potential cybersecurity risks associated with increased data processing.

name: Staffing and Training
consequences: Rotating research technicians for night shifts ensures adequate coverage, but it may lead to inconsistent annotation quality and reduced inter-rater reliability.
options: Implement a comprehensive training program for technicians on sleep disorder diagnosis and data annotation
Hire additional staff with expertise in sleep neurophysiology for enhanced data analysis
Develop a peer-to-peer mentoring system for technician training and knowledge sharing
review: This lever manages the tension between adequate staffing and consistent annotation quality, but the options overlook potential burnout risks associated with rotating shifts.

name: Participant Compensation
consequences: Offering per-night compensation may increase participant engagement, but it might also lead to biased participant selection and reduced generalizability.
options: Implement a tiered compensation system based on participant participation and data quality
Offer additional incentives for participants who contribute high-quality data or participate in follow-up studies
Develop a participant loyalty program with rewards for repeat participation
review: This lever governs the trade-off between participant compensation and study generalizability, but the options overlook potential participant exploitation risks.

name: Data Sharing and Governance
consequences: Limiting public data sharing to de-identified physiological and sensor data may ensure participant privacy, but it might also compromise the study's scientific impact.
options: Develop a transparent data sharing policy with clear guidelines for participant consent
Establish a governance framework for managing sensitive data and ensuring participant confidentiality
Create a public-facing dashboard for sharing anonymized data and research findings
review: This lever manages the tension between participant privacy and scientific impact, but the options overlook potential data misuse risks.

name: Expansion and Intervention Studies
consequences: Securing funding for expansion and intervention studies may enable the development of new treatments and interventions, but it might also compromise the study's primary aims and objectives.
options: Develop a phased expansion plan with clear milestones and deliverables
Establish a governance framework for managing expansion and intervention studies
Create a public-facing website for sharing research findings and promoting awareness about sleep disorders
review: This lever governs the trade-off between expansion and intervention studies, but the options overlook potential participant exploitation risks.

name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership
consequences: Partnering with a pharmaceutical industry may provide additional funding and resources, but it might also compromise the study's independence and scientific integrity.
options: Develop a clear governance framework for managing industry partnerships
Establish a transparent data sharing policy with clear guidelines for participant consent
Create a public-facing dashboard for sharing anonymized data and research findings
review: This lever manages the tension between industry partnership and scientific integrity, but the options overlook potential data misuse risks.

name: Participant Flow Optimization
consequences: Optimizing participant flow can reduce recruitment time, increase participant yield, and enhance data quality. However, it may also lead to increased participant dropout rates due to the complexities of the pre-admission pathway.
options: Streamline the pre-admission pathway by integrating clinical interviews with collateral history from bed partners or household members
Implement a tiered recruitment strategy targeting high-yield participants based on their parasomnia frequency and severity
Develop a participant-centric approach focusing on personalized support and education to improve participant engagement and retention
review: This lever governs the tension between streamlining the research process and ensuring participant comfort, but the options overlook potential biases in participant selection.

name: Sensor Data Integration
consequences: Integrating multiple sensor streams can provide a more comprehensive understanding of parasomnia patterns, but it may also increase data complexity and require additional computational resources.
options: Develop a hybrid approach combining low-burden monitoring with scheduled enhanced nights to balance participant comfort and data quality
Implement real-time data fusion techniques to integrate sensor streams from different modalities and improve event detection accuracy
Design a modular data architecture allowing for easy integration of new sensors or data streams as they become available
review: This lever addresses the trade-off between data richness and computational feasibility, but the options miss potential issues with data standardization.

name: Facility Expansion
consequences: Expanding the research facility can increase participant capacity, enhance research productivity, and promote scalability, but it may also require significant financial resources and introduce logistical complexities.
options: Develop a phased expansion plan focusing on incremental growth and staff training to ensure operational readiness
Implement a modular design approach allowing for easy addition of new suites or facilities as needed
Invest in technology-enhanced staffing solutions, such as remote monitoring or AI-assisted annotation tools
review: This lever governs the tension between growth and control, but the options overlook potential issues with staff burnout.

name: Neurological Screening
consequences: Implementing neurological screening can improve participant safety, enhance data quality, and promote research integrity, but it may also increase operational costs and introduce logistical complexities.
options: Develop a tiered screening protocol based on participant parasomnia frequency or severity
Invest in technology-enhanced screening solutions, such as AI-assisted annotation tools or remote monitoring systems
Explore alternative screening models, such as collaborative research with neurology departments
review: This lever addresses the trade-off between safety and efficiency, but the options miss potential issues with data standardization.

name: Participant Exclusion Criteria
consequences: Establishing clear exclusion criteria can improve participant selection, enhance data quality, and promote research integrity, but it may also compromise participant inclusion rates and introduce logistical complexities.
options: Develop a tiered exclusion protocol based on participant parasomnia frequency or severity
Invest in technology-enhanced screening solutions, such as AI-assisted annotation tools or remote monitoring systems
Explore alternative exclusion models, such as collaborative research with neurology departments
review: This lever governs the tension between inclusion and exclusion, but the options overlook potential biases in participant selection.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development
consequences: Developing event-triage algorithms can improve data quality, enhance research productivity, and promote scalability, but it may also compromise algorithm accuracy or introduce logistical complexities.
options: Implement a hybrid approach combining human scoring with AI-assisted annotation tools to balance accuracy and efficiency
Invest in technology-enhanced algorithm development solutions, such as machine learning or deep learning techniques
Explore alternative algorithm models, such as collaborative research with computational neuroscientists
review: This lever addresses the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, but the options miss potential issues with data standardization.
